q: The current flowing through an ac circuit is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>120</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. How long will the current take to reach the peak value starting from zero?
question: The current flowing through an ac circuit is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>120</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. How long will the current take to reach the peak value starting from zero?
solution:
If initial value of current is zero, then it will take T4 time to reach the peak value, where T= time period.

As we know, ω=120π

Therefore,

t=T4=2πω4  =π2ω=π2×120π=1240 s

question: When the rms voltages <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> are measured respectively across the inductor <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the capacitor <math> <mi>C</mi> </math> and the resistor <math> <mi>R</mi> </math> in a series <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></mrow> </math> circuit connected to an <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source, it is found that the ratio <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> <mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3.</mn></mrow> </math> If the rms voltage of the <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> is close to :
solution:
V L : V C : V R =1:2:3

V rms =100 V then V R =?



Let V L =x, then V C =2x and V R =3x

using  V= V R 2 + ( V c − V L ) 2
1 0 0 = 3 x 2 + 2 x - x 2

1 0 0 = 1 0 x 2




    x = 1 0 0 1 0 =10 10

∴    V R = 3 x  = 30 10 ≈90 V

If a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></math> is added to the circuit the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be :
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math>, power factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. If a capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub></math> is added to the circuit the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. solution:
In case of LR circuit:

Given: XL=R

P1=cosϕ=RZ=RR2+XL2=12

In case of LCR circuit:

P2=cosϕ=RZ=RR2+XL-XC2=1

Therefore,

P1P2=1:2

subject: Physics
q: An a.c. source of angular frequency $\omega$ is connected across a resistor $R$ and a capacitor $C$ in series. The current is observed as $I$. Now the frequency of the source is changed to $\omega/4$, (keeping the voltage unchanged) the current is found to be $I/3$. The ratio of resistance to reactance at frequency $\omega$ is
question: An a.c. source of angular frequency $\omega$ is connected across a resistor $R$ and a capacitor $C$ in series. The current is observed as $I$. Now the frequency of the source is changed to $\omega/4$, (keeping the voltage unchanged) the current is found to be $I/3$. The ratio of resistance to reactance at frequency $\omega$ is
solution:
The current in the RC circuit at frequency $\omega$ is given by

$I = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}}$

where $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ is the capacitive reactance.

When the frequency is changed to $\omega/4$, the new reactance becomes $X_C' = \dfrac{1}{(\omega/4)C} = 4X_C$.

The new current is given as $I/3$, so

$\dfrac{I}{3} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (4X_C)^2}}$

Substituting the expression for $I$, we get

$\dfrac{V}{3\sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + 16X_C^2}}$

Squaring both sides, we obtain

$\dfrac{1}{9(R^2 + X_C^2)} = \dfrac{1}{R^2 + 16X_C^2}$

$9R^2 + 9X_C^2 = R^2 + 16X_C^2$

$8R^2 = 7X_C^2$

$\dfrac{R^2}{X_C^2} = \dfrac{7}{8}$

$\dfrac{R}{X_C} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

Answer: $\sqrt{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>, a capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac source. If the current is in phase with the emf, the amplitude of current of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is-
question: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>, a capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> are connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> ac source. If the current is in phase with the emf, the amplitude of current of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is-
solution:
Here, emf and current are in phase, so the circuit is either pure resistive circuit or case of resonance,

XL=XC

Thus, the impedance of circuit is  Z=R2+XL-XC2=R=20 Ω

Now, rms value of current is irms=22020=11 A

Using the relation between peak and rms value of current, we have peak value i0=irms2=112=242 A.

Hence, the value of x=242.

subject: Physics
q: A series <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit containing an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> a capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>120</mn><mtext> μ</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and a resistor <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is driven by an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy dissipated in the circuit in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
question: A series <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> circuit containing an inductor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mH</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> a capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>120</mn><mtext> μ</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and a resistor <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is driven by an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> source of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy dissipated in the circuit in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is:
solution:
(Power factor), Pf=cos⁡ϕ=RZ

Where R, Z are the resistance and impedance respectively.
(Energy dissipated) E=Vrms2Z×cosϕ×t
⇒E=Vrms2Z×RZ×t

⇒E=Vrms2Z2×R×t

Vrms, t are the RMS voltage and time respectively.

We know that the Impedance of a LCR circuit is given by the formula,
     Z2=R2+ωL-1ωC2⇒Z2=602+2π×50×20×10-3-12π×50×120×10-62

Where ω, L, C are the angular frequency, inductance and capacitance in the circuit respectively.
⇒Z2=602+2π-100012π2

⇒Z2=4009.7

∴E=2424009.7×60×60 J 

= 517.14 J

=5.17×102 J

q:
An inductor of inductance 2 μH is connected in series with a resistance, a variable capacitor and an AC source of frequency 7 kHz. The value of capacitance for which maximum current is drawn into the circuit is 1x F, where the value of x is ______.

(Take π=227)
question:
An inductor of inductance 2 μH is connected in series with a resistance, a variable capacitor and an AC source of frequency 7 kHz. The value of capacitance for which maximum current is drawn into the circuit is 1x F, where the value of x is ______.

(Take π=227)
solution:
For maximum current in series LCR circuit,

XC=XL⇒12πfC=2πfL

⇒C=14π2f2 L=14×π2×49×106×2×10-6

⇒C=13872 F

Therefore, x=3872.

question: In a series resonant LCR circuit, the voltage across $R$ is 100 volts and $R=1 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ with $C=2 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The resonant frequency $\omega$ is $200 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. At resonance the voltage across $L$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{i}=\frac{100}{1000}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}$
$V_L=V_C=\frac{0.1}{200 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}=250 \mathrm{~V}$

q: The primary side of a transformer is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> supply. The turn ratio of primary to secondary winding is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Load resistance connected to the secondary side is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power consumed in it is:
question: The primary side of a transformer is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> supply. The turn ratio of primary to secondary winding is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Load resistance connected to the secondary side is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power consumed in it is:
solution:
The voltages associated with the primary and the secondary side of the transformer are related to the turn ratio as follows:

V1 V2=N1 N2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

230V2=101

V2=23 V

Hence, the power consumed can be calculated as follows

P=V22R=23×2346=11.5 W

subject: Physics
q: An inductor coil stores 64 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates energy at the rate of 640 W when a current of 8 A is passed through it. If this coil is joined across an ideal battery, find the time constant of the circuit (in s).
question: An inductor coil stores 64 J of magnetic field energy and dissipates energy at the rate of 640 W when a current of 8 A is passed through it. If this coil is joined across an ideal battery, find the time constant of the circuit (in s).
solution:
Energy stored in an inductor, U=12LI2
⇒64=12L(8)2 ⇒L=2 H
and power dissipated in a resistor, P=I2R
⇒640=(8)2R ⇒R=10 Ω
Time constant of the LR DC circuit is,
τ=LR=210=0.2 s

subject: Physics
q: A 110 V,50 Hz, AC source is connected in the circuit (as shown in figure). The current through the resistance 55 Ω, at resonance in the circuit, will be _____A.
question: A 110 V,50 Hz, AC source is connected in the circuit (as shown in figure). The current through the resistance 55 Ω, at resonance in the circuit, will be _____A.
solution:
Let current in the inductance is iL, through the capacitor is iC and through resistance is iR at any instance as shown in the figure.

According to the Kirchoff's junction law, iR=iL+iC.

At resonance XC=XL hence iL=iC. Also, current through the capacitor and inductor have phase difference of π rad. Therefore, they will form destructive interference and hence the current through the resistance will be 0.

q: In the given circuit, rms value of current Irms through the resistor R is :
question: In the given circuit, rms value of current Irms through the resistor R is :
solution:
Impedance of the given LCR circuit will be, Z=R2+XL-XC2⇒Z=1002+200-1002

=1002Ω

Therefore, RMS current can be written as,

Irms=VrmsZ=20021002

=2 A

subject: Physics
q: An inductor $(L=100 \mathrm{mH})$, a resistor $(R=100 \Omega)$ and a battery $(E=100 \mathrm{~V})$ are initially connected in series as shown in the figure. After a long time the battery is disconnected after short circuiting the points $A$ and $B$. The current in the circuit $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ after the circuit is
question: An inductor $(L=100 \mathrm{mH})$, a resistor $(R=100 \Omega)$ and a battery $(E=100 \mathrm{~V})$ are initially connected in series as shown in the figure. After a long time the battery is disconnected after short circuiting the points $A$ and $B$. The current in the circuit $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ after the circuit is
solution: $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{Rt} / \mathrm{L}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{A}$

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor $C$ is first charged fully with potential difference of $V_{0}$ and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor is connected across an inductor having inductance $L$. In $t \mathrm{~s} \ 25 \%$ of the initial energy in the capacitor is transferred to the inductor. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ s.
question: A capacitor $C$ is first charged fully with potential difference of $V_{0}$ and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor is connected across an inductor having inductance $L$. In $t \mathrm{~s} \ 25 \%$ of the initial energy in the capacitor is transferred to the inductor. The value of $t$ is $\_\_\_\_$ s.
solution:
In an LC circuit, energy oscillates sinusoidally.
Initial energy in capacitor is $E_0 = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$.
When 25% is transferred to the inductor, 75% remains in the capacitor.
Since energy in capacitor varies as $E_C(t) = E_0\cos^2(\omega t)$, we need $\cos^2(\omega t) = 0.75$.
This gives $\sin^2(\omega t) = 0.25$, so $\sin(\omega t) = 0.5$, yielding $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
With $\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$, we get $t = \frac{\pi\sqrt{LC}}{6}$ seconds.

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>210</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>3000</mn><mi>t</mi></math> volt is applied to a series LCR circuit in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The phase difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Φ</mi></mfenced></math> between the applied voltage and resultant current will be
question: A sinusoidal voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>210</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>3000</mn><mi>t</mi></math> volt is applied to a series LCR circuit in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The phase difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Φ</mi></mfenced></math> between the applied voltage and resultant current will be
solution:
Capacitive reactance is XC=1ωC=13000×25×10-6=403Ω

Inductive reactance is XL=ωL=3000×10×10-3=30Ω

Now, total reactance is X=XL-XC

=30-403=503Ω

tanϕ=XR=503100=16

tanϕ=16=0.17

Hence, the phase difference between the applied voltage and resultant current is tan-10.17.

subject: Physics
q: Primary coil of a transformer is connected to 220 V AC. Primary and secondary turns of the transforms are 100 and 10 respectively. Secondary coil of transformer is connected to two series resistances as shown in figure. The output voltage V0 is :
question: Primary coil of a transformer is connected to 220 V AC. Primary and secondary turns of the transforms are 100 and 10 respectively. Secondary coil of transformer is connected to two series resistances as shown in figure. The output voltage V0 is :
solution:
Ratio of EMF developed will be given by,

εpεs=NpNs=10010⇒εs=22 V

Therefore, current in secondary circuit will be

I=2222×103=1 mA

Hence, potential drop across 7 kΩ will be V0=I×7 kΩ=7 V

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal voltage $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=100 \sin (500 \mathrm{t})$ is applied across a pure inductance of $L=0.02 \mathrm{H}$. The current through the coil is:
question: A sinusoidal voltage $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=100 \sin (500 \mathrm{t})$ is applied across a pure inductance of $L=0.02 \mathrm{H}$. The current through the coil is:
solution:
In a pure inductive circuit current always lags behind the emf by $\frac{\pi}{2}$.
If $\mathrm{v}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{v}_0 \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$ then $I=I_0 \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ Now, given $v(t)=100 \sin (500 t)$ and $\mathrm{I}_0=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{\omega \mathrm{L}}=\frac{100}{500 \times 0.02}[\because \mathrm{L}=0.02 \mathrm{H}]$ $\mathrm{I}_0=10 \sin \left(500 \mathrm{t}-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$ $\mathrm{I}_0=-10 \cos (500 \mathrm{t})$

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> is connected to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery through a resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and a switch. After a long time, when maximum current is set up in the circuit, the current is switched off. The current in the circuit after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μs</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to ______ . (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>37</mn></math>)
question: An inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> is connected to a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery through a resistor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and a switch. After a long time, when maximum current is set up in the circuit, the current is switched off. The current in the circuit after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μs</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to ______ . (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>37</mn></math>)
solution:
Imax=VR=20 V10 KΩ=2 mA

For LR-decay circuit, 

I=Imaxe-Rt/L

I=2 mA e-10×103×1×10-610×10-3

I=2 mA e-1

I=2×0.37 mA

I=74100 mA

x=74
